schools.summary: The verse is a heading to the officer-lists; the stress on 'all Israel' betrays a writer compiling after the kingdom's disruption; the chapter belongs to the middle of the reign.
Gill: unlike David at first and his divided successors, Solomon reigned over all by consent, enthroned in the people's hearts through his wisdom.
Henry: Solomon was established king and did the work and duty of a king; those keep the honour of their office who mind its business.

The administrator of the kingdom heads the list, not the general as under David; 'kohen' may mean chief counsellor, and 'the priest' is added of Azariah by anticipation; peaceful offices now rank first.
Gill: the princes are the officers in the highest posts of honour and trust about the king.

debates.question: Does 'the priest' (ha-kohen) attach to Azariah as administrator, or to Zadok?

sides.position: Azariah is chief counsellor/administrator; 'the priest' names his grandfather Zadok or is used by anticipation
a caution that these 'princes' were chief men, not princes in the ordinary sense

schools.summary: The scribes and recorder held the same posts under David; Shisha = Shavsha = Sheva, the office becoming hereditary; the scribe issued edicts, the recorder kept the archives.
Gill: Shisha being dead, both sons were kept on as secretaries, Solomon's reign requiring more of the office.
Clarke: the scribes are secretaries, and Jehoshaphat the historiographer who chronicled the kingdom, in office already under David.

Abiathar, though deposed from the priestly office, is still titled priest because the priestly dignity was for life and hereditary — as Annas is called high priest in the Gospels; Benaiah now leads the host in Joab's place.
Gill: Benaiah is general of the army in the room of Joab.

debates.question: How can the deposed Abiathar still be called a priest?

sides.position: he lost the office (arche) but kept the priestly dignity (hierosyne), which was hereditary and for life

themes: settled order in sword and sanctuary

schools.summary: Zabud's title 'kohen' means privy councillor, not son of a priest; the Nathan meant is David's son, so Azariah and Zabud are Solomon's nephews; 'the king's friend' echoes Hushai's office under David.
Gill: Azariah was set over the twelve officers who provisioned the household.

debates.question: Which Nathan fathered Azariah and Zabud?

sides.position: Nathan the son of David, making them Solomon's nephews
Nathan the prophet (rejected)

schools.summary: Ahishar is the new office of governor of the palace; Adoniram (Adoram) over the tribute is head of the forced-labour levy for Solomon's works, later stoned in the revolt against Rehoboam.
Gill: Ahishar is steward of the household.
Clarke: Ahishar is the king's chamberlain and Adoniram a chancellor of the exchequer, both in office already under David.

themes: the seed of the kingdom's ruin

schools.summary: The twelve are chief tax-collectors gathering revenue in kind for the court; their districts cut across the old tribal boundaries, which had largely become obsolete for such purposes.
Gill: the twelve answer to the twelve months, each providing in his turn, not to the twelve tribes.
Henry: the scheme is wise husbandry — food brought from afar, trust divided among many, wealth spread into every tribe.
Five officers are named by their fathers with 'Ben' prefixed; the first district, mount Ephraim, is among the most fruitful and beautiful regions of the land, around Shechem between Ebal and Gerizim.
Gill: the fathers' names are given as being well known.
The second district is the maritime plain northwest of Judah, assigned to Dan but long held by the Philistines; its one famous city is Beth-shemesh, the ark's first resting-place on its return.
Gill: Makaz lay in the tribe of Dan, on its borders.

The third district lies in Judah's lowland toward Philistia; Sochoh stands near the field where David slew Goliath, and Hepher is an old Amorite city conquered by Joshua whose name survives for the region.
Gill: Aruboth lay in the tribe of Judah, judged by the towns that follow.

themes: the field of David and Goliath

schools.summary: The fourth district is the coastal range of Dor under Carmel, in Manasseh; its governor, probably Solomon's cousin, was married to his daughter Taphath, dating the list to the later reign.
Gill: the region of Dor and her towns belonged to the half-tribe of Manasseh.
Henry: to marry royal daughters to Israelite officers was wiser than matching them with heathen princes, strangers to the covenant.

The fifth and largest district is the plain of Jezreel/Esdraelon with Taanach, Megiddo, and Beth-shean — the battle-field of Deborah, Gideon, Saul, and later Josiah — now turned wholly to provisioning the king.
Gill: Taanach, Megiddo, and all Bethshean lay in the tribe of Manasseh.
The sixth district crosses Jordan into Bashan: Ramoth-gilead in Gad and the region of Argob (Trachonitis), the old giant-kingdom of Og with its sixty walled cities, the towns of Jair.
Gill: Ramoth-gilead was a city of Gad and a city of refuge.
Clarke: the 'brazen bars' were brass-plated gates, which he illustrates with Virgil's picture of Pyrrhus rending the brazen doors of Priam's palace.

outliers.note: Leaves the geography to quote Virgil's Aeneid on the brazen doors of Priam's palace as illustration of the cities' brazen bars.

schools.summary: The seventh district is Mahanaim ('the camps') north of the Jabbok in Gad — scene of Jacob's angelic vision, seat of Ishbosheth, and David's refuge from Absalom — now a fruitful province.
Gill: Mahanaim, where Ishbosheth and David once dwelt, must have been very fruitful to furnish a month's provision.

themes: provision where there was once flight

schools.summary: The eighth district is Naphtali, the upper Jordan valley south of Hermon with Hazor and Kedesh-Naphtali; its governor, possibly Zadok's son Ahimaaz, was married to Solomon's daughter Basmath.
Gill: Ahimaaz made his yearly month's provision out of the tribe of Naphtali.

themes: the frontier bound to the crown

schools.summary: The ninth district is oil-rich Asher along the Tyrian coast north of Carmel; its governor Baanah is probably the son of Hushai, David's faithful friend, though Asher had never fully occupied its allotted land.
Gill: Asher was plentiful, famed for oil; the officer was perhaps son of Hushai the Archite, promoted for his father's sake.

themes: a tribe that mingled with the Canaanites

schools.summary: The tenth district is Issachar in the plain of Jezreel, overlapping the fifth so closely the frontiers blur; its Manassite cities fall to Baanah, and tribeless Zebulun is folded in under a neighbour.
Gill: Jehoshaphat had the whole tribe of Issachar for his yearly month's provision.

The eleventh district is small but strong Benjamin, including Jerusalem, Jericho, Bethel, and Gibeon; its governor Shimei is possibly the loyal Shimei of 1 Kings 1:8, and is carefully distinguished from the Shimei who cursed David.
Gill: this Shimei is marked out to distinguish him from the Benjamite Shimei who cursed David.

The twelfth district is the whole land east of Jordan (Sihon's and Og's country) apart from the two earlier eastern districts; the odd closing clause either means one prefect for the vast region or, per the Greek, names a governor for the otherwise-omitted Judah.
Gill: the country of Gilead means all trans-Jordan Reuben, Gad, and half-Manasseh, excepting Ben-geber's district.

debates.question: What does 'he was the only officer in the land' mean?

sides.position: one prefect governed the whole vast eastern region despite its extent
the Greek reading points to a governor for Judah, which the list otherwise omits

themes: the land of Sihon and Og

schools.summary: Gill: the sand-like multitude is God's blessing fulfilling the promise to Abraham (Genesis 22:17), and an emblem of Christ's spiritual people in the latter day.
Keil: the words show the patriarchal promise of a numberless seed fulfilled; the ancient versions run the account on into the next chapter.
Henry: never was Israel's kingdom so glorious a type of the Messiah's, answering Psalm 72; the mirth is the enjoyment of settled peace.

The empire runs from the Euphrates to Philistia and Egypt's border; the tributaries 'brought presents.' Ellicott stresses it was a loose supremacy over tributaries, resting on wealth and personal ascendancy, quick to rise and quick to fall.
Clarke: the bounds show Solomon possessed all the land God covenanted to Abraham, with Syria, Damascus, Moab, and Ammon as tributaries.
Gill: he ruled all the surrounding kingdoms, who paid presents and tribute in homage.
Henry: the dominion from sea to sea answers Psalm 72 and types Messiah's reign; David won it by war, Solomon held it by wisdom.
The cor holds ten ephahs; from the flour Keil computes about 28,000 lbs of bread and a court of some 14,000, while Ellicott, cautious on the measure, agrees the household numbered several thousands.
Gill: the measures are cors (the homer), about seventy-five gallons each, showing the vast daily consumption.
Clarke: older reckonings (Vilalpandus, Calvisius) put those fed daily at the palace as high as 48,600 or even 54,000, including the guards.

debates.question: How many were fed daily at Solomon's table?

sides.position: about fourteen thousand, computed from the bread
as many as 48,600 or 54,000 on older estimates
probably several thousands; the measure is uncertain

schools.summary: Ten fatted oxen, twenty pasture oxen, a hundred sheep, and wild game make up the day's meat; Ellicott leaves the precise species open, taking the game as distinct from the herds and the 'fatted fowl' as dainty food generally.
Clarke parses each Hebrew term — tson (sheep and goats), tsebi (gazelle), yachmur (buffalo), barburim (creatures of the field) — concluding the table held all the house or field could afford.
Gill: the fat oxen were stall-fed and fattened.

debates.question: What exactly are the game animals named?

sides.position: specific species identified by their Hebrew roots (gazelle, buffalo, field-creatures)
wild game generally, the exact species left open; 'fatted fowl' = dainty food

themes: abundance of a land at rest

schools.summary: 'On this side the river' really means 'beyond the river' as seen from Babylon (cf. Ezra), betraying that the author wrote in the Exile; Solomon ruled from Tiphsah to Gaza and had peace on every side.
Gill: dominion beyond the Euphrates accounts for the plenty and revenue that upheld the royal table.

Keil: to sit under one's vine and fig tree is the enjoyment of the land's produce, taken up by the prophets as the emblem of the Messianic age (Micah 4:4; Zechariah 3:10); Clarke: the people no longer needed walled cities but spread over the land.
Gill: the people dwelt safely, without fear, a security more fully fulfilled in Christ, the antitype of Solomon.

themes: peace as type of the Gospel

schools.summary: 'Forty thousand' is a copyist's error for four thousand (cf. 2 Chronicles 9:25; 1 Kings 10:26); Ellicott adds that this multiplying of horses set aside the law of Deuteronomy 17:16 and made Solomon's realm like the Oriental monarchies.
Gill: reconciles the numbers by taking stalls versus stables (ten to a stable) or the whole kingdom versus Jerusalem, or a simple copyist's slip.
Clarke: the versions vary (LXX 'four thousand mares'); a corruption is clear, and he settles on four thousand stalls, 1,400 chariots, 12,000 horsemen.

debates.question: How is 'forty thousand stalls' to be reconciled with the 'four thousand' elsewhere?

sides.position: a copyist's error; read four thousand
harmonize by stalls versus stables, or the whole kingdom versus Jerusalem

schools.summary: Keil: the twelve prefects provided for the king and all who came to his table, each in his month, so that nothing was wanting — a further proof of the blessings of peace.
Gill: the officers are named again to show they provisioned the horses and fed not only the household but the strangers who came to see the court's splendour.

Barley was the ordinary provender and straw the litter; the officers brought them to wherever the horses were. Ellicott takes the 'dromedaries' (rechesh) as the swift horses of the royal messengers, distinct from the war-horses.
Clarke: rechesh means a swift race- or post-horse, needed to reach Solomon's many distant provinces.
Gill: barley was the common food of horses, as Bochart and the Mishnah attest, and the straw their litter.

sides.position: swift race- or post-horses of the royal couriers
a swifter sort of beast, the exact kind uncertain

themes: barley and straw for the horses

schools.summary: Keil distinguishes practical wisdom (chokmah), keen understanding (tebunah), and largeness of heart (capacity for diverse knowledge); Ellicott warns that wisdom is God's gift, begun in the fear of the Lord, and turns to self-idolatry if faith is lost.
Gill: the wisdom came not from genius or industry but as the gift of God, the fountain of all wisdom (Proverbs 2:6).
Henry: God gave it in answer to prayer; largeness of heart is a vast compass of mind and a free disposition to do good with it.
Clarke: Bacon's figure — as the shore's sand encloses the sea, so Solomon's mind held an ocean of knowledge.

themes: the fear of the Lord its root
the peril of wisdom without faith

schools.summary: The 'children of the east' are the Arabian tribes (Sabaeans, Temanites) and Chaldeans famed for gnomic wisdom and astronomy; Ellicott points to Job as a chief of the east, whose book displays that wisdom, linked with the proverbial learning of Egypt.
Gill: the children of the east are the Arabians and Persians, skilled in the stars and divination, matched against the wisdom of Egypt.
Clarke: Chaldeans, Persians, Arabians, and Egyptians were famed for wisdom through all the world, and Solomon excelled them.
Henry: as nature is excelled by art, much more the wisdom man attains by the wisdom God gives by special favour.

themes: Job and the wisdom of Egypt

schools.summary: Keil identifies Ethan, Heman, Chalcol, and Darda with the sons of Zerah (1 Chronicles 2:6); Ellicott and Clarke deny this, taking them as Solomon's contemporaries, the temple singers Heman and Ethan, and read 'Mahol' as 'music' rather than a father's name.
Gill: Solomon was wiser than all men since the fall of Adam, especially in natural wisdom.
Clarke: 'sons of Mahol' means masters of music or dancing, hinting at Solomon's own skill in music and poetry.

debates.question: Who are Ethan, Heman, Chalcol, and Darda?

sides.position: the sons of Zerah of Judah (1 Chronicles 2:6)
Solomon's contemporaries, the temple singers, 'Mahol' meaning music

schools.summary: Of the 3,000 proverbs only a selection survives in the Book of Proverbs, and of 1,005 songs only the Song of Songs and perhaps two Psalms; Ellicott makes this proof that the Canon is a deliberate, prophetically authorized selection from a large literature.
Gill: the spoken proverbs were lost in time, while the inspired Book of Proverbs was preserved.
Clarke: of over a thousand songs only the Song of Solomon remains, unless Psalm 127 be counted.

Cedar and hyssop are set in antithesis to span the whole vegetable kingdom; Ellicott weighs the allegorical (Josephus), magical (rabbinic), and scientific readings, finding Solomon's science joined to a contemplation of God in nature (Psalm 104).
Clarke: a complete system of natural history, Solomon the first natural historian, now wholly lost by God's providence, to the grief of later naturalists.
Gill: he spoke of all trees, herbs, and plants — their nature, virtues, and use.

debates.question: What was the character of Solomon's knowledge of nature?

sides.position: a lost empirical natural history
observation joined to contemplation of God in nature, tending to parable

themes: reading the creature back to God

schools.summary: Solomon's fame drew strangers, kings, and ambassadors from every quarter to Jerusalem, the queen of Sheba (1 Kings 10) being the recorded proof; later ages embellished the story with legend.
Henry: Solomon's court was the rendezvous of the learned, and he was herein a type of Christ, in whom are hid all the treasures of wisdom (Colossians 2:3), to whom the nations shall come for rest.
Gill: there came of all people to hear the wisdom of Solomon.

themes: type of Christ the Wisdom of God
all treasures of wisdom in Christ
